reflection.theme: The Saturday That Used to Be Lost
reflection.quote: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
reflection.reading:
Saturdays used to be a write-off. Either I was hungover from Friday or building toward another wreck by Sunday morning. The whole day was scaffolding for drinking — pick up beer, meet up with people who also drank, find a reason to start early, call it celebrating. I lost most of my Saturdays in my twenties to that same shape, and I couldn't have told you what I actually did with any of them.

Mid-May Saturdays now have texture. The light is longer. You can feel summer in the air without it being oppressive yet. Maybe you go for a hike, or you sit on a patio with coffee and actually taste it, or you say yes to something a friend invited you to two weeks ago and you remember to show up. None of this is glamorous. It's not what you sell people on. But it's what a life is actually made of — a stack of ordinary days where you were present enough to notice them.

The version of me at 22 thought sobriety would mean watching everyone else live. What it actually meant was getting my own Saturdays back. The small ones. The ones where nothing in particular happened and I was there for all of it. That trade looked impossible from the outside. From the inside, it's the only thing that ever made sense.
reflection.meditation: What does a present Saturday look like for you today? Not a perfect one — just one you'll remember being in.

affirmations: I am awake for my own life today, and that is not a small thing.
Today I get to choose what I do with these hours instead of losing them.
I am building a life I don't need to escape from, one ordinary day at a time.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a Saturday I'll actually remember. Grateful for the long light, for coffee that tastes like coffee, for plans I can keep and friends who expect me to show up. Grateful that the hardest years taught me what a quiet morning is worth.
gratitude.closing: One more day on this side of it. That's the whole gift.
